title: Genetic engineering and selective breeding
examTip: AQA tests both processes and expects you to evaluate them — not just describe what they are. For genetic engineering, know the mechanism (isolate → cut → insert → clone) and examples (insulin, golden rice). For selective breeding, know the process, benefits, and risks (reduced gene pool, inbreeding). Ethical arguments appear frequently in 6-mark questions.
introTips: Selective breeding is thousands of years old — it's how humans turned wolves into dogs and wild grasses into wheat. Genetic engineering is the modern, precise version.
Know a clear example for each — insulin from bacteria and golden rice for genetic engineering; disease-resistant crops and high-yield animals for selective breeding.
The evaluation angle is important: AQA wants pros AND cons of both, and may ask you to weigh up ethical concerns.
intro: Humans have been changing the genetics of other species for millennia — first through selective breeding (choosing which individuals reproduce) and more recently through genetic engineering (directly inserting genes from one organism into another). Both techniques raise important scientific and ethical questions that AQA loves to put in extended-answer questions.

keyPoints.heading: Selective breeding — choosing who reproduces
keyPoints.content: Selective breeding (also called artificial selection) is the process by which humans choose which organisms breed together in order to produce offspring with desirable characteristics. It has been used for thousands of years. Examples include: breeding cows that produce more milk, growing wheat with larger grain, developing disease-resistant crops, selecting dogs for specific traits (size, temperament). The process: identify individuals with the desired characteristic → breed them together → select the offspring with the best expression of the trait → repeat over many generations. Over time, the desired characteristic becomes more common in the population.
writeBullets: Selective breeding = humans choose which organisms breed to produce desirable traits
Process: identify desired trait → breed those individuals → select best offspring → repeat
Examples: high-yield dairy cows, large-grain wheat, disease-resistant crops, dog breeds
Has been used for thousands of years
Over generations: desired alleles become more common in the population

keyTerms.def: The process in which humans choose organisms with desired characteristics to breed together, producing offspring with those characteristics over generations
Another name for selective breeding — humans acting as the selecting force instead of the natural environment

keyPoints.memTip: Selective breeding = humans play nature. Instead of the environment choosing who survives, humans choose who breeds. The alleles for the desired trait increase in frequency generation by generation — the same mechanism as natural selection, but with humans deciding what's 'good'.

keyPoints.heading: Risks of selective breeding
keyPoints.content: Selective breeding reduces genetic diversity (also called the gene pool). When only certain individuals breed, many alleles are lost from the population. This creates several risks. Inbreeding: closely related individuals may be bred together, increasing the chance of inherited diseases (recessive alleles become more likely to pair up). Reduced adaptability: with less genetic variation, the population is less able to adapt if the environment changes (e.g. a new disease, climate change). A single pathogen can wipe out an entire crop of genetically identical plants. This is why seed banks and maintaining wild-type populations are important.
writeBullets: Selective breeding reduces genetic diversity — fewer alleles in the gene pool
Inbreeding: related individuals bred together → recessive disease alleles more likely to pair
Reduced adaptability: less variation = less ability to respond to new selection pressures
Monocultures: genetically similar crops vulnerable to the same disease
Seed banks help preserve genetic diversity of wild varieties

keyTerms.def: The range of different alleles within a population — essential for adaptability and long-term survival
Breeding between closely related individuals, increasing the risk of offspring inheriting two copies of harmful recessive alleles
A facility that stores seeds from diverse plant varieties to preserve genetic diversity for future use

keyPoints.memTip: Selective breeding reduces variation — that's the core risk. Less variation = less resilience. Think: if all wheat is genetically identical, one fungal disease could destroy the entire harvest. That's why preserving wild varieties and seed banks matter.

keyPoints.heading: Genetic engineering — inserting genes between species
keyPoints.content: Genetic engineering involves directly changing an organism's genome by inserting, deleting, or modifying genes. The key technique is inserting a gene from one organism into another. The process: (1) the desired gene is identified and isolated from a donor organism; (2) the gene is cut out using restriction enzymes; (3) the gene is inserted into a vector (usually a bacterial plasmid) using ligase enzymes; (4) the vector is introduced into the target organism (e.g. a bacterium), which then expresses the gene and produces the desired protein. AQA examples include: bacteria engineered to produce human insulin (for treating diabetes), crops engineered to be herbicide-resistant or pest-resistant, golden rice (engineered to produce beta-carotene/vitamin A).
writeBullets: Genetic engineering: directly inserting, deleting or modifying genes in an organism's genome
Process: identify desired gene → cut out (restriction enzymes) → insert into vector (plasmid) using ligase → introduce into target organism
Bacterium with human insulin gene → produces human insulin → used to treat diabetes
Golden rice: engineered to produce beta-carotene (Vitamin A) — helps prevent blindness in developing countries
GM crops: resistant to herbicides, pests, or disease

keyTerms.def: The process of directly altering an organism's genome, typically by inserting a gene from another species
Enzymes that cut DNA at specific sequences, used to isolate a desired gene
Enzymes that join pieces of DNA together — used to insert a desired gene into a plasmid
A small, circular piece of DNA found in bacteria, used as a vector to carry new genes into a host organism
Genetically modified organism — one that has had its genome altered by genetic engineering

keyPoints.memTip: The four steps: Identify → Cut (restriction enzyme) → Insert into plasmid (ligase) → Introduce to host. For AQA, the two classic examples are: insulin (bacteria produce human insulin for diabetics) and golden rice (produces beta-carotene for Vitamin A deficiency).

keyPoints.heading: Evaluating genetic engineering — benefits and concerns
keyPoints.content: Benefits include: producing medicines like insulin cheaply and without animal sources; creating crops with higher yields or nutritional value (golden rice); pest-resistant crops that require fewer pesticides; potential treatments for genetic diseases (gene therapy). Concerns include: modified genes could transfer to wild organisms (gene escape); reduced biodiversity if GM crops replace traditional varieties; ethical concerns about 'playing God' or modifying animals for human benefit; unknown long-term effects on ecosystems; some people object to consuming GM foods. AQA frequently asks you to evaluate — that means weighing up both sides and reaching a conclusion.
writeBullets: Benefits: produces insulin cheaply; improves crop yield/nutrition; reduces pesticide use
Golden rice provides Vitamin A in regions where deficiency causes blindness
Concerns: modified genes could escape into wild populations
Could reduce biodiversity if GM crops replace traditional varieties
Ethical: 'playing God'; unknown long-term ecosystem effects; consumer concerns about GM food

keyTerms.def: A potential medical treatment that involves inserting correct copies of a gene into a patient's cells to treat a genetic disease
Genetically modified crops — plants engineered to have traits such as pest resistance, herbicide resistance, or improved nutritional content

keyPoints.memTip: For evaluation: benefits = solve real problems (insulin, vitamin A, less pesticide). Risks = ecological (gene escape, biodiversity loss) + ethical (interfering with nature, unknown effects). Always give both sides and a conclusion for 6-mark questions.

tables.title: Selective breeding vs genetic engineering
rows: Fast — changes made directly to genome
Low — many genes affected at once
No — genes stay within a species (or close relatives)
Yes — genes transferred between any species
High-yield cattle, dog breeds, disease-resistant wheat
Insulin-producing bacteria, GM crops, golden rice
Gene escape, ethical concerns, unknown ecological effects

0.question: What is selective breeding?
options: Inserting a gene from one organism into another to give it a new characteristic
The process where humans choose organisms with desired characteristics to breed together over many generations
A natural process where the environment selects which organisms survive and reproduce
The use of restriction enzymes to cut out and isolate specific genes
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — selective breeding is human-controlled, choosing which individuals breed to develop desired traits over generations.
0.wrongFeedback: Selective breeding is the process where humans (not the environment) choose which organisms with desired characteristics are allowed to breed. This is done repeatedly over many generations until the desired trait is consistently expressed. It's different from genetic engineering (which directly modifies DNA) and natural selection (which is driven by the environment).

2.question: Which enzyme is used to cut a desired gene out of a donor organism's DNA?
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific sequences. Ligase then joins the gene into the plasmid.
2.wrongFeedback: Restriction enzymes are used to cut DNA at specific sequences — they isolate the desired gene. Ligase enzymes are then used to join the gene into the plasmid (vector). Remember: Restriction = cut, Ligase = join.

3.question: Which of the following is a concern about selective breeding (not genetic engineering)?
options: Genes may escape into wild populations
Reduced genetic diversity in the population due to only certain individuals breeding
Unknown effects of cross-species gene transfer
Consumers refusing to eat genetically modified organisms
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — selective breeding reduces the gene pool because only individuals with desired traits breed, which over generations reduces overall genetic diversity.
3.wrongFeedback: The key risk specific to selective breeding is reduced genetic diversity. Over generations, only selected individuals breed, so many alleles are lost from the population — making it more vulnerable to disease or environmental change. The other options (gene escape, cross-species effects, GM food concerns) are risks of genetic engineering, not selective breeding.

commonMistakes: Confusing selective breeding with natural selection — selective breeding is controlled by humans, choosing which organisms breed. Natural selection is controlled by the environment, with survival and reproduction determined by how well adapted an organism is.
Confusing restriction and ligase enzymes — restriction enzymes CUT (like scissors); ligase enzymes JOIN (like glue). The exam frequently tests which enzyme does which job.
Forgetting the plasmid (vector) step in genetic engineering — the gene doesn't go directly into the bacterium; it's inserted into a plasmid first, which then carries it into the bacterial cell.
Only giving benefits or only giving concerns in evaluation questions — 'evaluate' means both sides. Write 2-3 benefits AND 2-3 concerns, then a concluding statement.
Saying selective breeding causes 'bad mutations' — selective breeding doesn't cause mutations. It simply selects which existing alleles become more frequent.
examTips: For genetic engineering questions, learn the four-step process: identify → cut out (restriction enzyme) → insert into plasmid (ligase) → introduce to host. Each step may be worth a separate mark.

Know at least two specific examples of genetic engineering with the benefit: insulin (treats diabetes), golden rice (prevents Vitamin A deficiency/blindness), herbicide-resistant crops (less pesticide needed).
For 'evaluate' questions: give 2-3 concrete benefits, 2-3 concrete risks, then a balanced conclusion ('overall, the benefits/risks outweigh because...'). Never skip the conclusion.
When describing selective breeding risks, use 'reduced genetic diversity' not just 'reduced variation' — the term gene pool / genetic diversity is more precise and more likely to earn marks.
revisionCardBullets: Selective breeding = humans choose which organisms breed to develop desired traits
Repeated over generations → desired allele becomes more common
Risk of selective breeding: reduced genetic diversity, inbreeding depression
Genetic engineering: insert gene from one organism into another's genome
Steps: identify gene → cut (restriction enzyme) → insert into plasmid (ligase) → introduce to host
Insulin: human insulin gene inserted into bacteria → bacteria produce insulin
Golden rice: engineered to produce beta-carotene (Vitamin A)
GM crop benefits: pest resistance, herbicide resistance, higher yield, better nutrition
GM concerns: gene escape, reduced biodiversity, ethical issues, unknown ecological effects
Restriction enzymes = cut DNA; Ligase enzymes = join DNA
